questions.question: The dose rate is 120 mR/h at 5 feet from an exposed source. Ignoring scatter, the rate at 10 feet is about:
questions.explanation: Doubling the distance cuts the rate by 2² = 4: 120 ÷ 4 = 30 mR/h. Inverse square: I₁d₁² = I₂d₂².

questions.question: Using the rule of thumb of about 0.5 R/h per curie at 1 meter for Ir-192, an unshielded 80 Ci source produces about:
questions.explanation: 80 Ci × 0.5 R/h per Ci ≈ 40 R/h at 1 meter - a reminder of why distance and shielding are managed so aggressively around working sources.

questions.question: Bringing a dose rate down by a factor of 100 requires about:
questions.explanation: Each tenth-value layer divides the rate by 10, so two TVLs give a factor of 100; since one TVL ≈ 3.32 HVL, that is about 6.6 half-value layers.

questions.question: Restricted-area boundaries for a radiographic exposure are typically posted:
options: at a fixed 25 feet from the device on every job
wherever the barrier rope conveniently reaches
where the survey meter confirms about 2 mR/h or less
only when members of the public are nearby
questions.explanation: The boundary is a measured line: inverse-square math predicts where it will fall, but the posted line is set and verified with the survey meter at about 2 mR/h (typical practice), and re-surveyed when the setup changes.

questions.question: Which choice reflects ALARA thinking, rather than a bare regulatory minimum?
options: Stopping work only when a dose limit is exceeded
Wearing the badge because the license requires it
Posting the boundary exactly where the procedure minimum allows
Re-rigging the collimator and re-walking the crank position to cut a routine shot's dose, even though current doses are already legal
questions.explanation: ALARA means avoiding every reasonably avoidable millirem even when doses are already under the limits - limits are ceilings, not targets, and cheap reductions like collimation and crank placement are expected on every shot.

questions.question: Standing for 12 minutes at a position where the dose rate is 10 mR/h gives a dose of:
questions.explanation: Dose = rate × time = 10 mR/h × (12/60) h = 2 mR. Time spent in a field converts directly to dose, which is why waiting happens outside the boundary.

questions.question: During radiographic operations, typical regulations require each radiographer to wear:
options: a pocket dosimeter and a badge, with a survey meter somewhere on site
an alarming ratemeter only, when a survey meter is in use
a direct-reading dosimeter, an alarming ratemeter, and a processed badge - all three
questions.explanation: The typical 10 CFR 34-style rule is all three worn together: the ratemeter guards this second, the direct-reading dosimeter scores today, and the processed badge becomes the legal dose of record.

questions.question: Your pocket dosimeter is found off-scale at midday. The required response is to:
options: stop radiographic work, report it, and have the processed badge read promptly - your true dose is unknown
average the reading with yesterday's value
swap in a spare dosimeter and continue the shot list
questions.explanation: An off-scale direct-reading dosimeter means the accumulated dose is unknown; work ceases until the processed badge establishes the actual dose, per typical radiography regulations.

questions.question: In a gamma projector, the stored source capsule is shielded primarily by:
options: the collimator mounted at the exit port
a depleted-uranium or tungsten shield surrounding the capsule at the center of the S-tube
the lead lining of the transport case
questions.explanation: The dense DU or tungsten block does the shielding; the S-tube geometry keeps the capsule surrounded in every direction with no straight-line path to the outside. The thin outer shell is mostly structure.

questions.question: For an accurate boundary survey at radiography dose rates, the preferred instrument is:
questions.explanation: The ion chamber is linear and accurate across radiography-level fields. GM instruments can saturate and read low, scintillators are for very low levels and source searches, and the alarming ratemeter is a warning device, not a measuring instrument.
